content.text: A feature store is a data system purpose-built for serving pre-computed ML features to both model training (offline) and model serving (online), with guarantees of consistency between the two. Feature stores emerged from classical ML workflows where models consume structured numerical or categorical features — "user age", "account balance 30-day average", "number of purchases this week" — that must be computed once, stored at low latency, and retrieved consistently at both train-time and serve-time. In LLMOps, feature stores play a different but important role: rather than feeding model training directly, they supply structured context that is injected into LLM prompts to personalise or ground the model's responses.

attrs.alt: Feature store architecture: data sources → feature pipeline (batch + streaming) → offline store (historical) and online store (low-latency), with a feature registry for metadata and a serving API that injects features into LLM prompts.

content.text: The four core components of a feature store are:

: a columnar data warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake, S3 + Parquet) that holds the full historical record of every feature value for every entity. Used for model training (point-in-time joins) and for backfilling the online store.
: a low-latency key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB, Cassandra) that holds only the
value of each feature for each entity. Sub-millisecond lookup latency is the design target.

: the compute layer (Spark, Flink, dbt) that reads raw data sources, applies transformations, and writes feature values to both stores. Batch pipelines run on a schedule; streaming pipelines update the online store in near-real-time.
: the metadata catalogue that tracks each feature's schema, owner, lineage, freshness SLA, and documentation. Prevents duplicate feature engineering work and enables discoverability.
An entity is the primary key of the feature store — the object that features are computed about. Common entities are
. A feature value is always associated with an entity and a timestamp.
The most important property of a feature store is
: when training a model on historical data, the feature store must return the feature value that was
available at the time of the training example

, not the value that exists today. Returning the current value instead ("future leakage") causes models to train on data they could not have seen at prediction time, producing optimistically biased offline metrics and degraded online performance.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional

@dataclass
class FeatureValue:
    entity_id: str
    feature_name: str
    value: Any
    event_timestamp: datetime     # when the value became valid
    created_timestamp: datetime   # when it was written to the store

@dataclass
class HistoricalFeatureRequest:
    entity_rows: List[Dict[str, Any]]   # [{entity_id: ..., event_timestamp: ...}]
    feature_names: List[str]

class OfflineFeatureStore:
    """
    Simplified interface; real implementations (Feast, Tecton) run SQL against
    BigQuery or Snowflake with a point-in-time join.
    """
    def get_historical_features(
        self,
        request: HistoricalFeatureRequest,
    ) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
        """
        For each row in entity_rows, return the feature values that were valid
        at or before the row's event_timestamp.
        This is the point-in-time join that prevents future leakage.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError

Every feature has a freshness SLA: the maximum acceptable age of the value served to the online store. A "7-day rolling average spend" feature may tolerate being 1 hour stale; a "cart value" feature for a checkout model must be current within seconds. The feature pipeline schedule is designed to meet each feature's freshness SLA.

LLMs do not consume numerical feature vectors directly — they consume text. The feature store's role in LLMOps is therefore to supply structured, pre-computed context that the orchestration layer formats into the LLM prompt. This enables personalisation and grounding without requiring the LLM to compute facts from raw data.
: inject the user's subscription tier, preferred language, recent activity summary, and support history into the system prompt before every assistant response.
: inject the customer's risk score and account flags into a financial assistant's prompt so it can calibrate its advice appropriately.
: inject recently viewed products, purchase history summary, and category affinity scores into a shopping assistant prompt.
: retrieve the few-shot examples most relevant to the current user segment based on pre-computed segment features.

Commercial feature stores (Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks, AWS SageMaker Feature Store) carry significant operational weight. For teams that need only online serving of pre-computed features — the primary LLMOps use case — a lightweight implementation backed by Redis is often sufficient.
Feature Store vs Direct Database Queries
A common question is why to use a feature store at all — why not query the application database directly when building the LLM prompt? The feature store solves problems that direct queries cannot.
Each team rewrites the same transformations
Application DB credentials in serving path
Separate serving layer, no app DB exposure
Expensive at query time (SUM, AVG over rolling window)
When to Use a Feature Store in LLMOps

: the LLM system personalises responses based on user attributes that are computed from multiple data sources; the application serves thousands of users per minute and cannot afford per-request database joins; the same features are used by both LLM prompts and classical ML models.
: the LLM system serves a small number of internal users; features are simple and can be fetched from a single table with a primary key lookup.
: the LLM system is single-user or batch; personalisation is not a requirement; the context injected into prompts comes from the user's own session data (no pre-computation needed).

A feature store is justified when the same pre-computed entity attributes are needed at high throughput with sub-10ms latency and must be consistent between model training and serving. If any of those three requirements is absent, a simpler approach is probably better.
Teams that want full control; integrates with any cloud
Enterprises needing streaming features and SLA guarantees
Teams already on AWS with SageMaker pipelines
Teams needing only online serving; simple setup
Teams on GCP; tight BigQuery integration

Feature freshness is the time between when an event occurs in the real world and when the feature value reflecting that event is available in the online store. In LLMOps, a user's recent conversation history or subscription tier change affects prompt context immediately — stale features degrade personalisation quality.
Each feature group needs an explicit freshness target matched to its business semantics:
Wrong model tier routed → cost overrun
Feature Pipelines: Batch, Streaming, and On-Demand
Feature stores decouple feature computation from feature serving. Three pipeline styles cover different latency-freshness trade-offs:

# Pseudocode for a nightly batch pipeline updating aggregate features
# Typically run in Spark, dbt, or a SQL warehouse job

def nightly_user_feature_batch(date: str) -> None:
    """Compute per-user aggregate features and write to offline + online stores."""
    # 1. Read raw event log from data warehouse
    df = spark.sql(f"""
        SELECT
            user_id,
            COUNT(*) AS messages_last_30d,
            AVG(response_rating) AS avg_rating_last_30d,
            MAX(session_start) AS last_active_ts
        FROM llm_events
        WHERE DATE(session_start) BETWEEN DATE_SUB('{date}', 30) AND '{date}'
        GROUP BY user_id
    """)

    # 2. Write to offline store (e.g. Parquet on S3) with partition by date
    df.write.mode("overwrite").parquet(f"s3://features/user_aggregates/dt={date}/")

    # 3. Write latest values to online store (Redis) for real-time serving
    for row in df.collect():
        redis.hset(
            f"features:user:{row.user_id}",
            mapping={
                "messages_last_30d": row.messages_last_30d,
                "avg_rating_last_30d": row.avg_rating_last_30d,
                "last_active_ts": row.last_active_ts,
                "_event_ts": date,  # for freshness monitoring
            }
        )

# Kafka consumer that updates features within seconds of events
from kafka import KafkaConsumer
import json, redis

consumer = KafkaConsumer(
    "llm-events",
    bootstrap_servers=["kafka:9092"],
    value_deserializer=lambda m: json.loads(m.decode()),
)
store = redis.Redis(host="redis", port=6379)

def process_message_event(event: dict) -> None:
    """Update message-count feature incrementally on each event."""
    user_id = event["user_id"]
    key = f"features:user:{user_id}"
    pipe = store.pipeline()
    pipe.hincrby(key, "messages_session", 1)
    pipe.hset(key, "_event_ts", event["timestamp"])
    pipe.expire(key, 86400 * 7)  # 7-day TTL
    pipe.execute()

for msg in consumer:
    process_message_event(msg.value)
Some features cannot be pre-computed because they depend on the incoming request. These are computed inside the serving path and cached briefly:

import hashlib, time
from functools import lru_cache

def compute_query_complexity(query: str) -> dict:
    """On-demand feature: rough complexity score for routing decisions."""
    word_count = len(query.split())
    has_code = any(marker in query for marker in ["```", "def ", "function ", "SELECT "])
    has_math = any(marker in query for marker in ["∑", "∫", "equation", "formula"])
    complexity = (
        min(word_count / 50.0, 1.0) * 0.5
        + (0.3 if has_code else 0.0)
        + (0.2 if has_math else 0.0)
    )
    return {
        "query_complexity": round(complexity, 3),
        "word_count": word_count,
        "has_code": has_code,
        "has_math": has_math,
        "_event_ts": time.time(),
    }

# Short-lived cache (30 s) to avoid recomputing for identical queries
_cache: dict[str, tuple[float, dict]] = {}

def get_query_features(query: str) -> dict:
    key = hashlib.md5(query.encode()).hexdigest()
    now = time.time()
    if key in _cache and now - _cache[key][0] < 30:
        return _cache[key][1]
    features = compute_query_complexity(query)
    _cache[key] = (now, features)
    return features
Feature schemas change as models evolve. A new feature added to a group must not break existing consumers that read only the old features. Two patterns help:
New features are added with defaults. Old readers ignore unknown keys. Redis hashes naturally support this — readers call HMGET with exactly the fields they need.
When a feature's semantics change (e.g. normalisation scale shifts), publish under a new group name (e.g. user_v2) and migrate consumers incrementally.
Track which models/services consume each feature. When all consumers migrate, the old feature key can be deprecated and eventually deleted.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

def test_redis_feature_store_point_in_time_key():
    """Key must encode entity and timestamp for point-in-time reads."""
    store = RedisFeatureStore(redis_client=MagicMock())
    key = store._key("user", "u123", "subscription_tier")
    assert "user" in key
    assert "u123" in key
    assert "subscription_tier" in key

def test_online_feature_store_returns_none_on_miss():
    mock_redis = MagicMock()
    mock_redis.mget.return_value = [None]
    store = RedisFeatureStore(redis_client=mock_redis)
    result = store.read(OnlineFeatureRequest(entity_id="u999", feature_names=["x"]))
    assert result.values == {"x": None}
    assert result.missing == ["x"]

def test_freshness_monitor_flags_stale_feature():
    monitor = FreshnessMonitor(slas={"tier": 300})
    violation = monitor.check("tier", "u1", event_timestamp=time.time() - 400)
    assert violation is not None
    assert violation.staleness_seconds > 300

def test_freshness_monitor_passes_fresh_feature():
    monitor = FreshnessMonitor(slas={"tier": 300})
    violation = monitor.check("tier", "u1", event_timestamp=time.time() - 100)
    assert violation is None
Redis memory scales with (entity count × feature count × avg value size). A million users × 20 features × 50 bytes each ≈ 1 GB — affordable, but compress value types where possible.
Batch pipelines write to both offline and online stores. Use a single compute job that forks output to both destinations rather than running two separate jobs.
Set TTLs aggressively on online features that have a natural expiry (session data, short-horizon predictions). Uncapped feature stores bloat over time.
Offline store costs are dominated by storage (Parquet on S3 is cheap) and query compute (Spark/Athena on historical lookups during training). Partition by date to limit scan scope.

A feature store is a dual-store system (offline + online) that pre-computes entity features once and serves them at low latency to both model training and model serving. In LLMOps its role shifts from feeding numerical feature vectors to training pipelines toward supplying structured, pre-computed context that is injected into LLM prompts for personalisation and grounding. The key advantages over direct database queries are sub-5ms serving latency (Redis), point-in-time correctness for training data, feature reuse across teams, and built-in freshness monitoring. A lightweight Redis-backed implementation is sufficient for most LLMOps online serving needs; a full feature store framework earns its complexity when streaming feature computation, training/serving consistency, and multi-team feature reuse are all required simultaneously.
